questions.question:
The following numbers are inserted into an empty binary search tree in the given order: 10, 1, 3, 5, 15, 12, 16. What is the height of the binary search tree (the height is the maximum distance of a leaf node from the root)?
A. 2
B. 3
C. 4
D. 6
questions.to_find: Height of the BST after inserting 10, 1, 3, 5, 15, 12, 16

understand.plain: We insert numbers one by one into an empty BST following BST rules: values smaller than current node go left, values larger go right. The height is the number of edges on the longest path from root to any leaf node.

keywords.explain: A binary tree where for every node, all keys in the left subtree are smaller and all keys in the right subtree are larger.
keywords.example: Inserting 10 then 5: 5 goes left of 10 because 5 < 10.

keywords.explain: The maximum number of edges from the root to any leaf node (deepest node).
keywords.example: A single node has height 0; a root with one child has height 1.

understand.visual_alt: BST with root 10, left child 1, right child 15. Node 1 has right child 3, which has right child 5. Node 15 has left child 12 and right child 16. The deepest node is 5 at level 3.

given.aim: Build a BST by inserting 10, 1, 3, 5, 15, 12, 16 in order and find its height.

terms.example: 10 becomes root; 1 < 10 so goes left; 3 > 1 so goes right of 1
terms.connects: Determines the shape of the BST and hence its height

terms.meaning: Max edges from root to any leaf
terms.connects: We count levels: root is level 0, and we find the deepest leaf

terms.example: 15 > 10, so 15 goes to right of root
terms.connects: Guides placement of each inserted key

given.plan: Insert each key step-by-step using BST rules. Track the depth of each inserted node. The maximum depth reached is the height.

steps.title: Build the BST step by step
steps.formula_raw: BST insert rule: go left if key < node, go right if key > node
steps.apply:
Insert 10: root (depth 0).
Insert 1: 1 < 10, go left of 10 (depth 1).
Insert 3: 3 < 10 go left, 3 > 1 go right of 1 (depth 2).
Insert 5: 5 < 10, 5 > 1, 5 > 3 go right of 3 (depth 3).
Insert 15: 15 > 10, go right of 10 (depth 1).
Insert 12: 12 > 10, 12 < 15 go left of 15 (depth 2).
Insert 16: 16 > 10, 16 > 15 go right of 15 (depth 2).
steps.note: The path 10 -> 1 -> 3 -> 5 is the longest path with 3 edges.

steps.title: Determine height
steps.formula_raw: height = max depth of any leaf
steps.apply: Leaf nodes and their depths: 5 at depth 3, 12 at depth 2, 16 at depth 2. Maximum depth = 3.

solution.result: The height of the BST is 3. Answer: B. 3

questions.formula_note: BST insertion rule: smaller keys go left, larger keys go right. Height = maximum edge-distance from root to deepest leaf.

questions.question:
A program takes as input a balanced binary search tree with n leaf nodes and computes the value of a function g(x) for each node x. If the cost of computing g(x) is min(number of leaf-nodes in left-subtree of x, number of leaf-nodes in right-subtree of x), then the worst-case time complexity of the program is?
A. Theta(n)
B. Theta(n^2)
C. Theta(n log n)
D. Theta(n^2 log n)
questions.to_find: Worst-case time complexity of computing g(x) for all nodes in a balanced BST with n leaf nodes

understand.plain: For each internal node x in a balanced BST, computing g(x) costs the minimum of the number of leaf nodes in its left and right subtrees. We need to sum this cost over all nodes. Since the tree is balanced, both subtrees have roughly equal leaf counts, so min is roughly half. We sum these costs across all levels.

keywords.meaning: A BST where the left and right subtrees differ in height by at most 1, giving O(log n) height.
keywords.example: An AVL tree or complete binary tree is balanced.

keywords.meaning: For node x, the cost equals the smaller of the leaf counts in its two subtrees.
keywords.example: If a node has 4 left leaves and 4 right leaves, cost = min(4,4) = 4.

understand.visual_alt: A balanced BST where at each level k from the root (level 0), there are 2^k nodes, each with roughly n/2^(k+1) leaves per subtree. The cost at level k is 2^k * (n/2^(k+1)) = n/2 per level, and there are log n levels, giving total cost n/2 * log n = Theta(n log n).

given.aim: Compute total cost of evaluating g(x) for all nodes in a balanced BST with n leaf nodes.

terms.meaning: Number of leaf nodes in the balanced BST
terms.example: n = 8 means 8 leaves and 7 internal nodes

terms.meaning: A level at depth k from the root (root at k=0)
terms.example: Root is at level 0, its children at level 1
terms.connects: At level k there are 2^k nodes, each with n/2^(k+1) leaves per subtree in a balanced tree

terms.meaning: For a balanced tree, nodes at level k have approx n/2^(k+1) leaves per side, so cost per node = n/2^(k+1)
terms.example: At level 1 (2 nodes): each node has n/4 leaves per side, cost per node = n/4, total at level = 2*(n/4) = n/2
terms.connects: Each level contributes n/2 to total cost

given.plan: Analyze cost level by level. In a balanced BST with n leaves, height = log_2(n). At level k, there are 2^k nodes, each contributing min(n/2^(k+1), n/2^(k+1)) = n/2^(k+1) cost. Sum across all log n levels.

steps.title: Cost contribution at level k
steps.apply: At level k in a balanced BST with n leaves, each node x has approximately n/2^(k+1) leaves in each subtree. So cost(x) = min(n/2^(k+1), n/2^(k+1)) = n/2^(k+1). Number of nodes at level k = 2^k. Total cost at level k = 2^k * (n/2^(k+1)) = n/2.
steps.note: Every level contributes exactly n/2 to the total cost.

steps.title: Sum across all levels
steps.formula_raw: Total cost = sum_{k=0}^{log n - 1} (n/2) = (n/2) * log n
steps.apply: Height of balanced BST with n leaves = log_2(n), so there are log_2(n) internal levels. Total cost = (log_2(n)) * (n/2) = Theta(n log n).
steps.note: The level 0 (root) contributes n/2 as well (n/2 leaves per subtree in a tree with n leaves).

solution.result: Worst-case time complexity is Theta(n log n). Answer: C. Theta(n log n)

questions.formula_note: Level-by-level cost summation in a balanced binary tree: each of the log n levels contributes Theta(n) cost, yielding Theta(n log n) total.

questions.question:
Postorder traversal of a given binary search tree T produces the following sequence of keys: 3, 9, 10, 25, 27, 29. Which one of the following sequences of keys can be the result of an in-order traversal of the tree T?
A. 9, 3, 10, 25, 27, 29
B. 3, 9, 10, 25, 27, 29
C. 3, 10, 9, 25, 29, 27
D. 9, 10, 25, 27, 29, 3
questions.to_find: Which sequence is a valid inorder traversal of the BST whose postorder is 3, 9, 10, 25, 27, 29

understand.plain: In a BST, inorder traversal always produces keys in sorted (ascending) order. The postorder sequence is given; we need to find which option is the sorted order of these same keys. We just need to sort the given keys.

keywords.explain: Always visits nodes in ascending sorted order (left, root, right).
keywords.example: A BST with keys 5, 3, 7 gives inorder: 3, 5, 7.

keywords.explain: Visits left subtree, right subtree, then root (left, right, root). Last element in postorder is the root.
keywords.example: For tree rooted at 10 with children 5 and 15: postorder = 5, 15, 10.

understand.visual_alt: The keys from postorder are {3, 9, 10, 25, 27, 29}. Sorted ascending: 3, 9, 10, 25, 27, 29. This is the inorder sequence of any BST on these keys.

given.aim: Identify the valid inorder sequence for a BST whose postorder traversal is 3, 9, 10, 25, 27, 29.

terms.example: The root of the full tree is the last element: 29
terms.connects: The postorder determines the exact BST structure

terms.meaning: Inorder traversal of any BST yields keys in sorted ascending order
terms.connects: We just need to sort the given keys to find the inorder sequence

terms.example: Same keys appear in both postorder and inorder, just in different order
terms.connects: Sorted order gives the inorder directly

given.plan: Since inorder of a BST is always sorted, sort the keys {3, 9, 10, 25, 27, 29} in ascending order and match against the options.

steps.title: Identify the key set from postorder
steps.apply: The postorder gives keys: 3, 9, 10, 25, 27, 29. These same keys appear in the BST.
steps.note: Postorder last element (29) is the root of the BST.

steps.title: Inorder = sorted order
steps.formula_raw: Inorder(BST) = sorted ascending sequence of all keys
steps.apply: Sorting {3, 9, 10, 25, 27, 29} ascending gives: 3, 9, 10, 25, 27, 29. This matches option B.
steps.note: Options A, C, D are not sorted, so they cannot be valid BST inorder traversals.

questions.formula_note: Fundamental BST property: inorder traversal always produces keys in non-decreasing sorted order.

questions.question:
How many distinct binary search trees can be created out of 4 distinct keys?
A. 5
B. 14
C. 24
D. 42
questions.to_find: Number of distinct BSTs that can be formed from 4 distinct keys

understand.plain: The number of structurally distinct BSTs with n keys is the n-th Catalan number. For n=4 keys, we use the formula C(n) = C(2n, n) / (n+1) to compute the number of distinct BST structures.

keywords.explain: The n-th Catalan number C_n counts the number of distinct binary trees (including BSTs) with n nodes. Formula: C_n = C(2n,n)/(n+1).
keywords.example: C_3 = C(6,3)/4 = 20/4 = 5 distinct BSTs for 3 keys.

keywords.explain: Different BSTs have different structures. With the same set of n keys, different insertion orders can create different tree shapes.
keywords.example: Keys {1,2,3}: inserting 2,1,3 gives a balanced tree; inserting 1,2,3 gives a chain (right-skewed).

understand.visual_alt: For n=4 keys, the Catalan number C_4 = C(8,4)/5 = 70/5 = 14. This counts all distinct shapes of BSTs with 4 nodes.

given.aim: Count the number of distinct BST structures possible with 4 distinct keys.

terms.connects: Directly gives the answer for n=4
Alternative way to compute the Catalan number

given.plan: Apply the Catalan number formula C_n = C(2n,n)/(n+1) for n=4, or use the recurrence. Both give 14.

steps.title: Verify using recurrence
steps.note: Each term C_i * C_(n-1-i) corresponds to choosing a root with i nodes in left subtree and n-1-i nodes in right subtree.

solution.result: 14 distinct BSTs can be created from 4 distinct keys. Answer: B. 14

questions.formula_note: Number of distinct BSTs with n keys equals the n-th Catalan number: C_n = C(2n,n)/(n+1). For n=4, C_4 = 14.

questions.question:
You are given the postorder traversal, P, of a binary search tree on the elements {1, 2, ..., n}. You have to determine the unique binary search tree that has P as its postorder traversal. What is the time complexity of the most efficient algorithm for doing this?
A. Theta(log n)
B. Theta(n)
C. Theta(n log n)
D. None of the above, as the tree cannot be uniquely determined
questions.to_find: Time complexity of the most efficient algorithm to reconstruct a unique BST from its postorder traversal, given keys are {1, 2, ..., n}

understand.plain: Given the postorder traversal of a BST on elements {1, 2, ..., n}, we need to reconstruct the original BST. The key insight is that postorder uniquely determines a BST (unlike for general binary trees where both inorder and postorder are needed). We can reconstruct the BST by processing the postorder array once in O(n) time.

keywords.explain: Visits left subtree, right subtree, then root. The last element in postorder is the root.
keywords.example: Postorder of a BST rooted at 5 with left child 3 and right child 7: gives 3, 7, 5.

keywords.explain: For a BST, postorder alone is sufficient to uniquely reconstruct it, because the BST property (inorder = sorted) provides the equivalent of a second traversal. No separate inorder needed.
keywords.example: Postorder [1, 3, 2]: root is 2, keys < 2 in left subtree = {1}, keys > 2 in right subtree = {3}.

understand.visual_alt: Given postorder P of a BST on {1,...,n}: last element is root, elements less than root come before elements greater than root in the postorder. We process right-to-left (or use a stack-based O(n) algorithm) to reconstruct the tree in linear time.

given.aim: Find the time complexity to reconstruct the unique BST from its postorder traversal on keys {1, 2, ..., n}.

terms.meaning: Array of n elements representing postorder traversal of the BST
terms.connects: Each element must be placed exactly once, so O(n) reads are needed at minimum

terms.meaning: A BST is uniquely determined by its postorder traversal (inorder is always sorted, so we get it for free)
terms.example: Postorder [1, 3, 2]: root=2, left keys < 2: {1}, right keys > 2: {3}. Tree is unique.
terms.connects: We do not need additional information — postorder alone suffices

terms.meaning: Process postorder right-to-left maintaining a stack; insert each element using BST rules. Each element is processed once.
terms.example: Read last element as root, partition remaining into left/right groups using BST property, recurse
terms.connects: Since each of n nodes is processed once, total time is Theta(n)

given.plan: The most efficient algorithm scans the postorder array once (reading n elements), so the lower bound is Omega(n). A stack-based algorithm achieves Theta(n). The tree is uniquely determined, so option D is wrong.

steps.title: Show the BST is uniquely determined by postorder
steps.formula_raw: Inorder(BST) = sorted order; postorder + inorder uniquely determines any binary tree
steps.apply: For a BST on {1, 2, ..., n}, the inorder traversal is always 1, 2, 3, ..., n (sorted). Since we have both inorder (implicit, always sorted) and postorder (given), the BST is uniquely determined. So option D is incorrect.
steps.note: Unlike general binary trees, BST postorder alone suffices because inorder is always the sorted sequence.

steps.title: Determine time complexity of optimal reconstruction
steps.apply: We must read all n elements of the postorder array at minimum, so time >= Omega(n). An efficient algorithm processes each node once: scan postorder right-to-left, maintaining a stack to track the current insertion path. Each node is pushed/popped at most once. Total time = Theta(n).
steps.note: A naive recursive algorithm that searches for the split point for each root would be O(n^2) in the worst case, but the stack-based approach achieves O(n).

solution.result: The most efficient algorithm runs in Theta(n) time. Answer: B. Theta(n)

questions.formula_note: A BST on {1,...,n} is uniquely determined by postorder alone (inorder is implicitly sorted). Stack-based reconstruction from postorder runs in Theta(n) time, processing each node exactly once.
